stories.headline: Denver Mayor Johnston Orders Police to Protect Anti-ICE Protesters, Detain Agents Using Excessive Force
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston issued an executive order Thursday directing city police officers to intervene and detain federal immigration agents who use excessive force against protesters. The order also bans ICE agents from using city-owned property for their operations and instructs officers to provide protection for peaceful demonstrators during immigration enforcement actions.

The directive represents one of the most aggressive municipal responses yet to the Trump administration's immigration crackdown and places Denver police in a potential confrontation role with federal agents. Johnston has previously positioned Denver as a sanctuary city resistant to federal immigration enforcement priorities.

The executive order raises significant legal and jurisdictional questions about the extent of local authority over federal law enforcement operations. It is unclear how or whether the order would be enforced if ICE agents declined to comply with detentions by city officers.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis Moves to Drop Nine-Month Effort to Comply with ICE Employment Records Subpoena
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis filed a motion Friday in Denver federal court seeking to permanently block release of state employment records that U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement had subpoenaed roughly a year ago. The records covered employment details for sponsors of unaccompanied immigrant children lacking legal status, and Polis had spent nine months attempting to comply with the subpoena before reversing course.

Polis' attorneys argued that ICE had not intervened in the related lawsuit nor taken any other action to enforce the subpoena, suggesting the agency either no longer needed the information or no longer considered its production urgent. A spokesman for the governor said the administration wants to "move on" from the matter.

The lawsuit was originally filed by a former state Department of Labor and Employment worker, Scott Moss, who sued last June to block compliance. The state employees union Colorado WINS and nonprofit law firm Towards Justice also joined the suit. Moss' attorneys said they had not yet agreed to the governor's proposal, noting they needed more time to respond substantively to the motion.

stories.headline: Update: Thornton Grass Fire Injures Five, Damages Businesses Before Containment
stories.summary:
A vegetation fire that ignited at approximately 11:30 a.m. Wednesday at 84th Avenue and Huron Street in Thornton injured five people — four firefighters and one civilian — before being fully contained at about 2:07 p.m., Thornton Fire Chief Stephen Kelley said at a press conference. The fire burned approximately 10 acres along a green belt between a residential neighborhood and a commercial zone under Red Flag conditions, prompting evacuations and road closures including a stretch of Interstate 25.

Kelley described the blaze as a "dynamic, rapidly spreading event" that required a large-scale mutual aid response involving Thornton, Westminster, Adams County, North Denver Metro, Northglenn, Federal Heights, South Adams and other agencies. A nearby high school was also evacuated as a precaution. No residential structures were lost, though some businesses on the southern edge of the fire sustained damage.

All five injuries were described as non-critical. Crews remained on scene overnight to monitor for any reignition. The cause of the fire is under investigation.

stories.headline: El Paso County Jury Convicts Man of Second-Degree Murder in 2024 Stabbing of UCCS Professor
stories.summary:
An El Paso County jury found Ceasar Wilson, 54, guilty Thursday on all charges, including second-degree murder, in the 2024 stabbing death of University of Colorado Colorado Springs professor Haleh Abghari. The verdict came after deliberations following a trial in the Springs-area case that drew widespread attention in Colorado's higher education community.

Abghari was killed in 2024 in an attack that shocked the UCCS campus and prompted discussions about faculty safety. Wilson faced the full range of charges brought by prosecutors, and the jury rejected any lesser findings.

The conviction closes a case that has been closely watched across the state. Sentencing proceedings are expected to follow.

stories.headline: Denver Downtown Development Authority Hires Urban Land Institute Panel to Chart Future of Denver Pavilions
stories.summary:
The Denver Downtown Development Authority will pay the Urban Land Institute $100,000 to convene an advisory panel that will visit Denver April 13-17 and produce recommendations on the future of the Denver Pavilions mall and adjacent parking lots at 500 16th St. The DDDA purchased the 350,000-square-foot mall for $37 million and two nearby parking lots for $23 million in December after the previous owner defaulted on its loan.

The mall is approximately 60 percent leased and generates $5.3 million in annual rent, with the parking facilities adding another $3.2 million per year. The DDDA has also issued two requests for proposals — one seeking retail and entertainment leasing specialists and another seeking architecture and urban planning firms — with submissions due March 13, ahead of the ULI panel's visit.

Denver Chief Projects Officer Bill Mosher said he hopes to transfer the property to private ownership as quickly as possible rather than sign major leases under public ownership. The ULI panel, which will consist of five or six members from outside the region, is expected to deliver its report approximately one month after the April visit.

stories.headline: Nuggets' Jamal Murray Cleared to Play Against Celtics Despite Hamstring Tightness
stories.summary:
Denver Nuggets All-Star guard Jamal Murray was upgraded to available Wednesday for the team's nationally televised home game against the Boston Celtics after being listed as probable with right hamstring tightness. Murray, averaging 25.5 points, 4.4 rebounds and 7.5 assists per game this season, has not missed a game despite the nagging injury.

The Nuggets entered the matchup having lost six of their last nine games and all seven of their most recent contests against teams with winning records. Key contributors Aaron Gordon, Peyton Watson and Jalen Pickett remained sidelined. Forward Julian Strawther, averaging 15.5 points over his last six starts, was also upgraded to available after being listed probable with a toe sprain.

The Celtics, despite missing Jayson Tatum for the entire season, rank among the NBA's elite behind Jaylen Brown's MVP-caliber campaign and Derrick White's consistent two-way play. A home win against Boston would have represented a significant statement for a Nuggets team under pressure to reverse its recent slide.

stories.headline: 303 Day, Petite Parade and Purim Drag Queen Bingo Highlight Denver's Weekend Events
stories.summary:
Denver's annual Buy Colorado Day — formerly known as 303 Day and recognized as a state holiday since 2025 — returns Tuesday, March 3, with bars, restaurants and boutiques across the metro offering themed products and special pricing including $3.03 cocktails. Indie 102.3 FM will host a free all-ages concert from 5 to 9 p.m. at Number Thirty Eight featuring local artists Bruha, Graveyard Choir and Cheap Perfume.

The eighth annual Dairy Block Petite Parade takes place Sunday, March 1, in LoDo from noon to 2 p.m., featuring miniature Mardi Gras-themed floats built on shoebox bases. The free event at the Dairy Block's heated alley on the 1800 block of Wazee Street includes prizes for Most Original Float and Best Team Theme.

The Mizel Arts and Culture Center's sixth annual Purim Drag Queen Bingo runs Saturday, Feb. 28, from 7:30 to 10:30 p.m. at the Staenberg-Loup Jewish Community Center, with a Garden of Eden theme, costume contest, cash bar and vegetarian appetizers. Tickets range from $50 to $75.

stories.headline: Mountain Snow Forecast Tops One Foot as Winter Storm Warning Issued for Northern Ranges
stories.summary:
The National Weather Service issued a winter storm warning covering parts of Jackson, Larimer, Grand and Boulder counties from Tuesday evening through late Wednesday, forecasting more than a foot of snow on Colorado's highest peaks and mountain passes. The strongest snowfall window was expected between 5 p.m. Tuesday and 4 p.m. Wednesday, accompanied by winds gusting up to 65 mph that will reduce visibility and create difficult travel conditions.

A separate winter weather advisory was issued for parts of Grand, Boulder, Gilpin, Clear Creek, Summit and Park counties — including Breckenridge, Winter Park and Berthoud Pass — covering late Tuesday night through late Wednesday night. No snow was forecast for the Denver metro area.

The storm arrives amid a historically low snowpack season statewide. Mountain communities and ski resorts are expected to benefit from the accumulation, though travel over high mountain passes is advised against during the warning period.

stories.headline: Sandoitchi Japanese Sandwich Pop-Up Returns to Denver's RiNo District Through March 7
stories.summary:
Sandoitchi, a Dallas-based pop-up known for elevated Japanese milk bread sandwiches called sandos, has returned to Denver for a two-week run at The Source Hotel in the River North Arts District, open daily from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. through Saturday, March 7. The pop-up, which last visited Denver in 2024, is led by chef Stevie Nguyen, a veteran of Morimoto and Momofuku Ko in New York.

The menu features returning favorites including the Strawberry Cream Sando, Egg Salad Sando and Classic Chicken Katsu Sando alongside new offerings such as a Wagyu Burger Katsu Sando, Curry Ebi Shrimp Katsu Sando, Elote Corn Karaage and Japanese Potato Croquette. Japanese-inspired beverages including matcha drinks, a Yuzu Arnold Palmer and a Manjo Ube Lassi are also available.

Sandoitchi sandos are made from crustless Japanese milk bread prepared using the tangzhong technique, which involves cooking flour and liquid into a roux before adding it to the dough to achieve a notably soft texture. The pop-up is one of several touring food concepts that have chosen Denver as a regular market stop.

stories.headline: Historical Data Shows Double-Digit March Snowstorms in Denver Are Not Uncommon Despite Dry Winter
stories.summary:
At least one long-range weather modeling service, Pivotal Weather, is projecting up to 14 inches of snow for Denver during the first half of March, a forecast that has drawn attention amid one of the driest winters on record for the city. However, historical National Weather Service data shows that March is actually Denver's snowiest month on average, with a normal accumulation of approximately 11.4 inches.

Records dating to 1882 show that Denver has received 16.5 inches or more of snow in a single day on 10 occasions, four of which occurred in March, including a 19.9-inch storm on March 14, 2021, the third-highest single-day total on record. The current 14-inch forecast refers to a multi-day accumulation rather than a single event, a threshold that falls well short of the 20 largest storms in Denver history, all of which exceeded 17.3 inches.

Other forecasting services, including AccuWeather, project temperatures in the 50s and 60s during the same period, raising uncertainty about whether conditions will support significant snowfall. Meteorologists note the forecast remains highly uncertain at current lead times.
